text: today. The clear tendency in the reception and application of liminality in the social and cultural sciences, in art and performance, in business and consultancy, is that liminality represents an unordered, chaotic element of creativity and freedom in a modern world that was drowning with too much rationality. Liminality is adopted as fitting within a larger framework of social constructivism, or as resonating with post-structuralist theories, positing themselves against more structural or nominalist philosophies or world-views. As we have argued throughout, there are compelling reasons to reject this reception altogether, and in a most fundamental way. Social constructivism developed as a paradigm in order to stress human agency in the meaningful construction of the ‘world’ as we see and live and act within it. As a paradigm, it presented itself as an alternative and correction to more dominant rationalist-positivist scientific views, from rational choice theory in political science to realism in Liminality and the Modern 228 international relations. And yet rational choice theory and social constructivism share a foundational epistemology: namely that the world itself is essentially unordered, and that human beings impose their order upon it via their (rational or not, individual or social) choices, ideas and acts. Rationalism and social constructivism alike are predicated upon a prior distancing to the world. They merely perpetuate a kind of epistemic homelessness which goes to the heart of modern thinking. This is certainly not where Arnold van Gennep belongs. For van Gennep the basic fact of transition somehow tied human beings and ‘culture’ to nature. Transitions from group to group or from one social situation to the next are a ‘fact of society’, van Gennep said (1960: 3). However, for van Gennep transitions are also a ‘fact of nature’, and in a sense it was this view he made explicit in Rites of Passage. As he introduces the notion of transitions, he claims that ‘[t]he universe itself is governed by a periodicity which has repercussions on human life, with stages and transitions, movements forward, and periods of relative inactivity’ (ibid.). Van Gennep somehow bases his entire world-view on a certain degree of homology or at least coincidence between social and natural rhythms. It is also this vision which brings him into close proximity with Tarde’s sociology. The world may seem chaotic, but in actual fact, Tarde says, the social world, just like nature, is dominated by regularities. The task of science is not to impose some order on phenomena from the outside through abstract, mental, cognitive operations, but rather to recognize the character and principles of this order. In doing so it is helped by the fact that the nature of this order is fundamentally identical in the diverse orders of reality: it is based on regularity and repetition. In the inorganic world repetitiveness is based on periodic and vibratory movements like waves; in living beings on heredity and genetics, while in social life on imitation (see Tarde 1903, Chapter 1). These repetitive regularities are the basis of both order and change. The various orders of reality are built one upon the other, and do not simply replace and cancel each other. In one of the unfinished notes he made for what was intended to the general conclusion to his multi-volume work, Manual of French Folklore, van Gennep explains that the system of classification that he adopted is ‘rhythmic’ (see Belmont 1979: 98). Ritual stages play such a fundamental role in social life ‘because life itself progresses by oscillations and by stages’ (ibid.). This view should easily be seen as a mature statement relating back to what van Gennep – when he was still engaged with sociology and anthropology – had tried to establish as a ‘life science’, studying ‘living facts’. The human being, from such a view, had to be studied as a part of nature. This was never meant as a reductionism, as we see so often in the recurrent attempts to explain human and social phenomena with reference to ‘nature’ or ‘biology’; almost quite the contrary: it comes much closer to an appreciation of forms, those ‘patterns that connect’, and which Bateson always defined as his real objects of study. The recognition of such rhythms, and even their re-presentation in textual form was for van Gennep tied to an aesthetic By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 229 dimension, which he indicated with surprising explicitness in another of his notes, left-behind and picked up by Belmont, which is worth quoting at length: A folklorist, unless he is a pure theoretician and has never undertaken investigation in the field, is necessarily sensitive to lines (forms) and colours, to music and sounds, to others’ psychic shifts and changing expressions of them. What one might call professional contact with vital rhythms reacts on the rhythm of one’s own writing; sympathy with the living is unconsciously expressed in the contouring of one’s sentences ... (ibid.: 100) Van Gennep returned to this underlying vision in the final pages of Rites de Passage, ending on the beautiful and significant closure of the book: Finally, the series of human transitions has, among some peoples, been linked to the celestial passages, the revolutions of the planets, and the phases of the moon. It is indeed a cosmic conception that relates the stages of human existence to those of plant and animal life and, by a sort of pre-scientific divination, joins them to the great rhythms of the universe. (van Gennep 1960: 194) 5 The style and content of this closing paragraph closely resembles the cosmology found in Plato’s Timeus, the work in which Plato most clearly posits the recognition of the beauty and order of the natural world as the condition for living and thinking rationally. Nature must here be understood in the best of classical traditions as a meaningfully ordered and animated universe, rather than a chaos upon which we, human beings, must impose our order. In normative terms, this can also be said differently: the role of human beings in the universe is not
